title: Listening - Part 3: Getting News
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about how they prefer to get news. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main advantage each speaker sees in their preferred source. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about how they prefer to get news. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main advantage each speaker sees in their preferred source. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about how they get news.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: I don't want to be constantly checking news websites, as I find that distracting and often depressing. However, I do need to know about events that might affect my day, such as transport problems or serious storms. [19]I use an alert service on my phone because it sends a message only when there is significant breaking information, which means I find out quickly without spending hours reading every small update.[/19] If a story interests me, I can look for further detail later.||Speaker 2 Man: National television news is useful, but it rarely tells me what is happening in the streets and schools close to where I live. I follow an online newspaper for my area. [20]Its website allows me to choose reports about my neighbourhood, including road closures, council plans and events at local centres, rather than searching through news from places many miles away.[/20] I discovered a community market through it last month and also learned in time about planned repairs on my usual bus route.||Speaker 3 Woman: I take an underground train to work, and there isn't a reliable internet connection for much of the journey. I used to open online articles before leaving home, but often forgot. [21]Now I pick up a free newspaper at the station, because I can read it throughout my commute even when my phone has no signal.[/21] It doesn't cover every issue in depth, but by the time I arrive at work, I usually know the main stories people are likely to be talking about.||Speaker 4 Man: When I first started following the news, I would read quick summaries on social media. I knew what had happened, but rarely understood the reasons behind it or what might happen next. [22]I now listen to a weekly radio programme that spends twenty minutes on one topic, gives the background and explains complicated details clearly instead of rushing from story to story.[/22] It requires more time, but it means I can form an opinion based on more than a headline.||Speaker 5 Woman: My parents have strong opinions on current events, and dinner conversations can sometimes sound as if only one position is sensible. I wanted to hear beyond that. [23]I follow an interview podcast where people who disagree are invited to explain their reasons calmly, so I can compare several viewpoints before deciding what I think.[/23] Sometimes I still agree with my parents, and sometimes I don't, but at least I understand why an issue can look different to different people.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: I don't want to be constantly checking news websites, as I find that distracting and often depressing. However, I do need to know about events that might affect my day, such as transport problems or serious storms. I use an alert service on my phone because it sends a message only when there is significant breaking information, which means I find out quickly without spending hours reading every small update. If a story interests me, I can look for further detail later.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: National television news is useful, but it rarely tells me what is happening in the streets and schools close to where I live. I follow an online newspaper for my area. Its website allows me to choose reports about my neighbourhood, including road closures, council plans and events at local centres, rather than searching through news from places many miles away. I discovered a community market through it last month and also learned in time about planned repairs on my usual bus route.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: I take an underground train to work, and there isn't a reliable internet connection for much of the journey. I used to open online articles before leaving home, but often forgot. Now I pick up a free newspaper at the station, because I can read it throughout my commute even when my phone has no signal. It doesn't cover every issue in depth, but by the time I arrive at work, I usually know the main stories people are likely to be talking about.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: When I first started following the news, I would read quick summaries on social media. I knew what had happened, but rarely understood the reasons behind it or what might happen next. I now listen to a weekly radio programme that spends twenty minutes on one topic, gives the background and explains complicated details clearly instead of rushing from story to story. It requires more time, but it means I can form an opinion based on more than a headline.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: My parents have strong opinions on current events, and dinner conversations can sometimes sound as if only one position is sensible. I wanted to hear beyond that. I follow an interview podcast where people who disagree are invited to explain their reasons calmly, so I can compare several viewpoints before deciding what I think. Sometimes I still agree with my parents, and sometimes I don't, but at least I understand why an issue can look different to different people.

options.text: It allows them to select local stories.
It is available during their journey.
It lets them hear different views.
It is easier to understand in another language.
It allows them to discuss stories with family.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The phone alerts inform the speaker rapidly about major developments without constant news checking (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The online source lets the speaker focus on relevant news from the immediate local area (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: A printed newspaper is convenient to read on an underground journey without internet connection (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The weekly radio programme gives the speaker background and fuller explanations of news topics (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The podcast allows the speaker to consider contrasting opinions on news issues (evidence [23]).
